description: Immerse yourself in the vibrant traditions of the Lunar New Year with this beautifully crafted email template. Perfect for celebration announcements, holiday greetings, and news updates, it effortlessly encapsulates the rich Chinese culture. The design is simple yet striking, dominated by auspicious red and yellow hues that symbolize luck and prosperity in China. Whether you're part of the media and entertainment industry, running a blog or magazine, or simply want to share the joy of the New Year, this template is a fantastic choice. Its traditional elements will add a unique touch to your communications while staying true to the spirit of the Chinese New Year.
short_description: Celebrate the Lunar New Year with this vibrant email template. Perfect for holiday greetings and news updates, its red and yellow hues embody luck and prosperity. Ideal for media, entertainment, blogs, or simply sharing joy. Add a unique, traditional touch to your communications with this Chinese New Year template.

designer.description: Hi! My name is Martin. I’m a web / user interface designer located in Bulgaria, Europe. Since the past 10 years I’ve worked on a variety of projects for small, medium and large companies. I enjoy doing pixel perfect designs with attention to every detail.

HAPPY NEW

YEAR 2020 

Hey, John we greet you

About Chinese New Year

In China, you'll hear it being called chunjie (春节), or the Spring Festival. It’s still very wintry, but the holiday marks the end of the coldest days. People welcome spring and what it brings along: planting and harvests, new beginnings and fresh starts. You can also call it the Lunar New Year, because countries such as North and South Korea and Vietnam celebrate it as well. And because the Spring Festival goes according to the lunar calendar. Which means ...

Learn More

There's no set date for Chinese New Year

In China, you'll hear it being called chunjie (春), or the Spring Festival. It’s still very wintry, but the holiday marks the end of the coldest days. People welcome spring and what it brings along: planting and harvests, new beginnings and fresh starts. You can also call it the Lunar New Year, because countries such as North and South Korea and Vietnam celebrate it as well. And because the Spring Festival goes according to the lunar calendar. Which means ...

Learn More

Chinese New Year Dishes

Spring rolls

Eggrolls are probably the most well-known of Chinese cuisine. However, they’re actually “spring rolls.” They are eaten during the Spring Festival in Southern China to celebrate the coming of spring. More specifically, they are eaten on the first day of spring (立春—lì chūn). They can appear on the table as a dinner dish, appetizer or snack.

Learn More

Dumplings

Another well-known dish, dumplings are the northern equivalent of spring rolls.

 

They are eaten during every special occasion, but are the most significant during Chinese New Year. That’s a lot of dumplings! But there’s good reason.

Learn More

Noodles

In some places, it’s custom to cook dumplings and noodles together. This is called gold silk and gold ingots. It’s yet another dish to express people’s wishes for prosperity.

 

Though the origin of noodles is heavily disputed, there’s no doubt that noodles are a staple in Chinese cuisine.

 

In some places, it’s custom to cook dumplings and noodles together. 

Learn More

Last Year Party

title: Chinese New Year
